pr_review_body: Awesome fix.
LGTM

Can split this PR into two pieces? 

We need to settle on the requirements for the learner. I think there is so many changes proposed to the learner at the same time, it is making the learner API complex again. 

Read my suggestion from https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/35385#discussion_r1195942859  We can define a simple and yet effective stats reporting API inside the base learner class. `self.record_metrics/stats(key, value)`. With this you can get rid of all the stats dicts that you have to return. We will also define a getter method (`self.get_metrics(filter_fn=None)`) which will allow people to get metrics and apply custom filtering function on the fly. 

Also it makes the API so much nicer and cleaner. Let's do that in a separate PR first and then build the DreamerLearner API on top of it.

I would love it if we could make this change, but unfortunately, this approach does not work with the way that we determine the version of a Node package prior to publishing. We derive the version of each package from the Git tag associated with the commit being published, and we use `${VERSION}` as a reliable pattern in `package.json` that allows us to stamp in the released version.

All that said, I do think that we could probably fix things here with a little bit of work on our end. @stack72 what do you think about using `pulumictl` to deserialize `package.json` and stamp in the correct version prior to publishing?

Last question:

Right now we have `meta=None` warn, saying that the default will be to infer in the future. How does a user get that infer behavior today without a warning? I don't think they can (short of silencing the warning) since Dask uses `None` to mean infer.

So I think we need the actual default to be

```
_no_default = object()

class ParallelPostFit:
    def __init__(..., meta=_no_default):
        if meta is _no_default:
            warnings.warn(...)
        self.meta = meta
```

so that we can distinguish a user explicitly opting into inference from a user who just hasn't specified anything.


But do we even care about that case? I'm not sure, since I think the transform will essentially always fail on a length-0 input since sklearn does length checks... I'm not sure, but I think it's best to make this change.

> Maybe consider preserving the FromNumpy vs FromNumpyRefs distinction, but I could see it either way.

We didn't distinguish FromNumpy and FromNumpyRefs before either. Because what `from_numpy` does is simply `put` + `from_numpy_refs`. Thus, I think it's not very useful to use `FromNumpyRefs` for the name, and just changed it to `FromNumpy`.

We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered in go1.19 when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, the deadlocks would still have been happening, leaking resources, but functionality would not have been impacted, only performance.

Thanks for putting this together, @richardliaw!  This is a good start.

I think there are a couple things we can do to address this SSH issue, one short-term and one long-term.

For an immediate workaround, we can allow the user to specify the `nics` and avoid using SSH to find the common interface.  This should already be handled by `get_common_interfaces` [here](../../ludwig/models/new_ludwig_model.py), just need to test that it works.

The more general solution is to find the common interfaces without requiring passwordless SSH.  This should also be doable, but will require more work.  Finding the common interfaces essentially consists of three parts:

1. Launch task servers on every worker host as shown [here](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/master/horovod/runner/driver/driver_service.py#L49).
2. Have every worker probe the next worker using its set of interfaces to determine which are "routable". This is shown [here](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/master/horovod/runner/common/util/network.py#L193). This is necessary because some interfaces will show up on all hosts but will not actually be capable of routing traffic on the network (e.g., `lo`).
3. Intersect the routable interfaces from each worker to find a common set, then we can choose any among these and be sure it will work.

My thinking is that we should be able to use the existing `get_common_interfaces` code, but swap out the part where we instantiate the task servers (see [here](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/master/horovod/runner/driver/driver_service.py#L105)). Instead of using SSH, we can use Ray primitives to create the servers via dependency injection, or via environment similar to how we handle Kubeflow in `get_remote_command`.  

What do you think?

One issue with unions (and a use case for which they were often used in the past) is as a means of conversion, e.g. between floats and their binary representations e.g. via your IntFloat class. This is all undefined behavior these days, and an explicit `memcpy` must be used.

That said, as long as it is clear that pybind11-bound enums should never be used for such conversion, I don't see a major issue in shipping this change. the change to `is_accessible_base_of` by @bstaletic is really harmless.

This is problematic for a number of reasons:

1. obviously, you forgot the other branch of the conditional (with profiling enabled, there's also a `processor.process()`...)
2. your cached processors were all constructed with a non-null `workspace` argument, which causes them to `chdir` into that:
https://github.com/OCR-D/core/blob/9ae6ddfc8798a07bced98e1d1c110e514c058bfd/ocrd/ocrd/processor/base.py#L140-L142
3. your current `workspace.directory` might not be an absolute path; so it would become relative to the previous workspace, not to CWD of the caller

So either you rebase to the `processor.old_pwd`, or pass `workspace=None` when caching, or resolve to absolute here before calling.

(BTW, in my [PR](https://github.com/OCR-D/core/pull/652) I also moved the chdir to `run_api` alone, which would be the equivalent of the second option I mentioned.)

There were two possible solutions proposed by Tom on the issue that motivated this PR.

> - We could potentially add some public API on the application so that it's easier to premptively resolve the request routing? Ideally we ensure that can be cached, so that the lookup only occurs once.
> - We could add the ability to have some kinds of middleware run after the request routing. (Probably not super keen on that.)

I took my time to think about the first solution, and how that would look like... I think what we can do is to add a parameter on the `Middleware` class:

```python
app = Starlette(
    middleware=[
        Middleware(PotatoMiddleware, path_prefix="/potato")  # or `path_prefixes`
    ]
)
```

Considering the above, we stick with the middleware concept being only at the app level.

Thoughts?

The working of the seed parameter can be checked with the following example:
```
import random

import networkx as nx
from node2vec import Node2Vec
import numpy as np

Will try to restate my previous review at a higher level. Ultimately, I'm worried that it's too difficult to reason about this interface, because it's too difficult to really understand the details right now. I think there are really 2 approaches to making it more understandable:
1. Flush out the exact semantics of the API, the meaning of the fields, and the interaction/error model with the relevant systems we interact with.
2. Make sure everyone is on the same page about the principals of the design (ideally by stating them) so that the exact semantics and failure model are well defined.

(2) is probably more productive. 

In particular, I think we're close to following a few principals that would give us a safe system (and it was my understanding that we were originally attempting to follow them).

========= These 2 comments apply both to `autoscaler_state_service.proto` and `instance_manager.proto` ==============

* Abstraction: The autoscaler should only know relevant parts of the scheduler state, but not necessarily details about how it evolves. Practically, the main fix here is would be to avoid guaranteeing implementation details about the version (like a "monotonically increasing ..."). In fact we may even find `int64` to be a limiting implementation detail since versioning could be per-raylet (and we've discussed getting reports directly from raylet before). Concretely, I believe changing the version type to `bytes` and changing the guarantee to "If 2 states have the same version, they are equivalent/interchangeable for autoscaling purposes" would fix the issue.
* E2e principle: The autoscaler operates at a higher layer than the cluster. Node launching functionality can and must be implemented at the autoscaler layer therefore the scheduler interface shouldn't interpret it.

========== This final comment primarily applies to instance_manager.proto =========

* Optimistic concurrency control: The instance manager is quite close to relying on optimistic concurrency control to ensure that autoscaler decisions are carried out safely (e.g. on the expected state). For this to work, we need to check that the instance manager state hasn't changed. Again, concretely one way to achieve this would be by defining a "version" concept (which can be typed as `bytes`) in which any two states with the same "version" are equivalent for autoscaling purposes. If the versions are different, we don't need to define the behavior if the versions are different, so long as the version changes less frequently than the decision making time.
* Simplicity: Perhaps this is a matter of taste, but I prefer it when interfaces are as simple as possible, therefore, it would be nice to not include fields if we don't know their purpose.

### Questions:

What I am wondering is: when a user initiates multiple calls to the server how is it ensured that the metsfile is accessed in a "synchronized way". It would be preferred for the implementation to support any trace-level tag but the logic to identify trace-level tags doesn't exist. Future work should be done to define trace-level or chunk-root-level tags in either the frontend or the backend of the library (probably the backend as it's dependent on the protocol to the agent/backend).

The only open question for me is why we don't have any overloads for `add_attachment` and the init of the attachment or maybe I'm missing something. 

Either you want to pass bytes for an attachment or a file to read. For the first method, you are forced to pass `bytes` and a `filename` for the second you only need to pass a `path`. This forces you to use the API right at compile time and you don't find out at runtime if you passed in the wrong arguments. We could ditch raising `TypeError` in the init of the `Attachment`. What do you think @mitsuhiko about that?

```
def add_attachment(
    self,
    bytes=None,  # type: bytes
    filename=None,  # type: str
    content_type=None,  # type: Optional[str]
    add_to_transactions=False,  # type: bool
)

def add_attachment(
    self,
    path=None,  # type: str
    filename=None,  # type: Optional[str]
    content_type=None,  # type: Optional[str]
    add_to_transactions=False,  # type: bool
)
```

This is super cool and overall I like the idea but am a little concerned about the magic, particularly for debugging if/when something goes wrong and for new users to the library.

I'm not sure it's intuitive to a new user if/what/how the code runs in the subprocess (scope, environment used). Having the code in a string indicates that it's scoped outside the current program with a given environment.

It's really nice that we can lint/format subprocess test code but I think we'll need to `# noqa` quite often since there could be variable scoping and import issues.

Also, by having to parse and compile the code we distance ourselves from running code as it would run in the real world (something might run fine in this but not in reality) which was one of the primary motivations behind subprocess testing.

You can add a class in `StatusWidget` (the parent element) within the [constructor](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab-git/blob/master/src/components/StatusWidget.tsx#L27) by adding the following line:

```ts
this.addClass('jp-git-StatusWidget')
```

Note: by design it is not possible to select parent of an element with CSS selectors.

> Another issue is when navigate to another repo (change file directory), the branch name stays unless you click on the git extension icon on the left menu bar, which is a bit weird.

The default behavior is to not refresh the Git model status when the sidebar is hidden to avoid useless calls to the backend. You could solve this by forcing refreshing the branch calling [GitExtension.refreshBranch](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab-git/blob/master/src/model.ts#L981) when we set a [new repository](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab-git/blob/master/src/index.ts#L151).

@zoccoler, thank you very much for your work so far and pushing this forward. 

> Should we not try and broadcast mask to input array?

I don't quite understand how this would help. As I understand the issue, SciPy's QHull doesn't like that there is a dimension of length 1, fails and `convex_hull_image` returns an empty image without a surface. 

I'd rather prefer to have this edge case supported upstream, than code around it in our function. However, I'm really unsure if this is something that would get resolved by upstream in a reasonable time frame. The [error is raised in qhull](https://github.com/qhull/qhull/blob/c2ef2209c28dc61ccfd22514971236587e820121/src/libqhull/geom2.c#L1283-L1284) which is in turn wrapped by SciPy. And it's way easier on our side to check for a dimension of size 1 than do it once it's transformed to coordinates.

So in the meantime I've left a few comments; feel free to address them or wait for what SciPy says...

GH has a custom format for defining the citation info: https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/managing-your-repositorys-settings-and-features/customizing-your-repository/about-citation-files

TL;DR you add a `CITATION.cff` file to the root of the project and GH adds a button on the top right column of the project home like they have in PyTorch core

![Screenshot from 2022-10-04 11-34-54](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6849766/193786473-b464189c-dbcb-4315-922f-204086915e48.png)

PyTorch core just defines a `CITATION` file, which is why there is no information rendered directly. If you follow their protocol, the prompt looks like 

![Screenshot from 2022-10-04 11-36-39](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6849766/193786815-63f94860-d90b-4ead-b6c3-a101f8efd419.png)

The use case that @Eric-Arellano highlights — needing to make a very small number of overall changes to support different Mac OS architectures — is something that I'd use immediately if we introduced it into Pants mainline and would simplify my dev environment, but I don't think we want to get to the point where we're introducing well-defined environments for every other platform we support in Pants. I think adopting environments in their entirety would be overkill for most users in this situation. We should let users adopt incrementally here.

The subsystem redesign allows users to adopt as much or as little of the target environments support as they need, and I don't think there's a good reason to arbitrarily stop people from using just a tiny bit of the functionality.

Did a usage first pass, I have a few high-level pubsub design questions before I dig deeper.

It looks like we're using a channel abstraction in order to be able to piggyback off of shared publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections, where the publisher and subscriber interfaces expose the channel concept to the application code via the channel type and deal only with a generic `PubMessage` message type that oneofs across the messages for the different channels. This then entails pass-through methods to the subinterfaces for each channel, maintaining channel --> data structure maps internally, and leaking what is essentially a transport detail (the long-polling connection) to the application level by requiring publishers/subscribers to specify the channel type. I'm wondering if there's a better way to structure this such that the application code doesn't have to worry about channels and more of this logic is encoded in the type system so we have compile-time guarantees that things are 👌 .

What if we adopted a pattern similar to gRPC's shared transport channel, generalized from managing a 1-to-1 connection to managing 1-to-n connections, where we have a transport broker concept that encapsulates the publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections. The subscriber interface for each pubsub instance takes a shared pointer to the broker on construction, and can therefore share the broker across different pubsub instances (e.g. separate subscriber objects for `WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION` and `WAIT_FOR_REF_REMOVED`), where new publisher <--> subscriber connections can be registered by any of the pubsub instances at subscription time.

```
subscribers <--> broker <--transport--> broker <--> publishers
```

I think there could be a few advantages here:

1. We could then expose a subscriber interface per pubsub instance, templated on the concrete message type (no need for a proto oneof message to be exposed to the application code, that could be isolated to the broker), which will give better compile-time checking of the application-level pubsub messages and will obviate the need for those passthrough methods in a top-level subscriber interface that requires a channel type to be specified.
2. I think that you'd still need a proto-level channel type enum, but I think that you could get away with specializing the subscriber with _the enum value itself_. E.g. `Subscriber<typename MessageID, typename Message, rpc::ChannelType channel_type>` could be specialized as `Subscriber<ObjectID, rpc::WaitForObjectEvictionMessage, rpc::WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION>`, where the `channel_type` is included when interacting with the broker (e.g. subscribe, unsubscribe). Dispatch of messages received in the long-poll response by the broker will still have to use a channel --> subscriber table, but that can be populated dynamically when the subscriber is constructed: `broker_.RegisterSubscriber(channel_type, this)`.
3. The transport-level optimization of sharing long-polling connections is pushed down as an implementation detail of the transport broker concept, and is a natural place to evolve other transport-level details, like switching to a bidi streaming connection or switching to a centralized message broker.
4. Other transport-level things, like the core worker client pool and the subscriber address/port, can be moved to the broker. Publisher addresses will probably continue to be required by subscriber methods, unless we want the broker to always map worker or node IDs to concrete addresses (probably less efficient in some cases?).
5. The current process of registering a new channel will be replaced by adding a new channel type to the proto enum, adding the channel's proto to the broker's pubsub message oneof, templating a subscriber with a new message type and the channel type, and reusing the existing broker. I don't think there should be much more required than that.

I think that you would have an analogous broker abstraction on the publisher side as well (encapsulating the cached long-polling connection, controlling the message batching), although I haven't thought it through as much since I think it should be simpler than the subscriber-side. My guess is that the publisher would be similarly templated, would similarly register itself with the publish-side broker on construction, and each publisher instance (one per channel type) would have its own subscription index. I think that the publisher's view of the subscription, `Subscriber`, would be internal to the publisher broker.

The cons with this broker approach, as I see them:

1. Broker concept is exposed to the application level, although what's needed to configure that concept (worker client pool, subscriber address/port, etc.) is already exposed to the application level, so I'm not sure if this is any worse?
2. A shared transport-level broker will obviously have to be thread-safe, although that shouldn't be very difficult.
3. Hidden dragons that I haven't thought of.

Lmk what you think about this approach and whether I'm missing anything! This is also something that could wait until a future PR, e.g. when I port the OBOD object location subscriptions or the GCS node/actor tables to this pubsub I'm sure that I'll have to refactor some stuff anyway.

In addition to my documentation nitpick, I have three higher level concerns about this after digging in more closely:

1. I think we should probably modify `certbot.hooks.post_hook` to accept domains lists instead or as well. Otherwise, post hooks will get these environment variables when using `certbot renew` but not with other subcommands.
2. I worry a bit about the list of domains becoming too long to shove in the OS environment. Imagine you're a shared hosting provider for many thousands of domains and many of these certificates try to renew at once. Any ideas on how we can eliminate/reduce the potential of breaking Certbot in this scenario?
3. I think we should add a simple unit or integration test for this feature so we don't accidentally break it in the future. Integration tests can be found under the top level `certbot-ci` directory.

What do you think about requiring users to return an `OutputsByPartition` object in this situation (which contains a dictionary of partition keys to values)? That would eliminate the possibility of confusing edge cases where an individual output value is a dictionary.

E.g. the outputs are JSON blobs. The user code only handles a single partition, but they try to materialize a range of partitions. They get a confusing error message saying '"some_field_in_json_blog" is not a valid partition key'. We could provide a more clear error message: "IOManager operating on a range of partitions, but returned output value only corresponds to a single partition. Return OutputByPartitions instead."
